key_insight: Increasing argon flow rate from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produced thicker, denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with better tribological performance.
engineering_relevance: Optimizing plasma-gas flow can directly improve coating quality, wear resistance, and service life for self-fluxing thermal-sprayed surface protection.
method: Air plasma sprayed NiCrFeBSiC self-fluxing metal-ceramic coatings
Compared argon flow rates of 1600 and 1800 L/h
Evaluated thickness, porosity, microhardness, elastic modulus, friction, and scratch adhesion
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased by about 2×, porosity decreased by about 2×, and the friction coefficient dropped to approximately 0.107 with stable scratch failure behavior.
watch_item: The study compares only two argon flow settings, so broader process optimization would need more gas-flow levels and additional durability testing.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
